title: Chapter 6
text: Chapter Three T he woman’s skirts swept around her legs as she spun with a grace Ronin doubted he could replicate. Her entire body moved as one, though the individual parts belied each other’s motions. It was a contradiction — subtle but powerful, wild and yet restrained . Though incomplete, his memory retained a surprising amount of information on humans — including details of their anatomy; the muscles, tissue, and skeletons that enabled their movement. Paired with his knowledge of basic physics, he should have understood why she was able to move like that . He’d seen other humans dance, in other settlements, but he’d never seen anyone move the way she was now. Knowledge of the underlying physical systems and unseen natural forces at work did nothing to explain how she was capable of such unpredictable, mesmerizing movement . This woman presented a new mystery, a new puzzle — human grace. Her dance lacked precision, but it was made up for by a raw, powerful quality Ronin could not adequately define . He studied her face as she turned again. Her eyes were closed, her pink lips turned down in a frown. Loose strands of her damp, red hair hung in her face, brushing over her freckle-dusted cheeks. Her lower lip quivered infinitesimally, coinciding with a brief break in her humming . She hesitated for a fraction of a second before resuming her dance . Ronin stepped closer to the door, leaning in until he was nearly touching it. The wind picked up, the gentle tinkling of the chimes interrupted by harsh clanging as several of the pieces hit his rucksack . The woman froze, pale blue eyes locked on Ronin . They stared at each other for six seconds before she finally spoke. “Who are you ?” Placing a hand on the edge of the door, Ronin slid it aside, and lifted a foot across the threshold . Her eyes flickered to his fingers, going wide, and she leapt back. With a fumbling hand, she reached behind her and picked up a steel bar. “ Get out !” “You asked who I am,” he said, pausing midstride in the doorway, booted foot hanging in the air . “I know what you are.” She raised her weapon with both hands, knuckles white. “You’re a bot. Get out !” “Haven’t come in yet .” Possibilities spiraled through his CPU, attributed arbitrary probabilities as they passed. A bot could guess what a human might do, but it was impossible to develop a definitive answer. Regardless, she’d only manage — at worst — a single swing. He was already due for repairs. One more dent wouldn’t change that . “You’re not welcome here, bolt bucket .” “You asked who I am, not what .” “I don’t care who you are .” “Then why bother asking at all ?” Her feet were planted wide apart, her stance defensive. A faint tremor ran through her arms as she adjusted her grip on her improvised weapon . The muscles of her jaw clenched and relaxed. “Because I thought you were human .” Pink blossomed on her cheeks, and she broke eye contact for a moment. Her weapon sagged. Fear, fatigue, or resignation ? “Would you dance again?” he asked . When she looked up at him again, her expression had changed, lips pressed into a tight line and brows low. “You’re shitting me, right? No. You want dancing, go to Kitty’s. I don’t fucking do that anymore .” Kitty’s. He knew of it, but had never gone inside. Had he missed the opportunity to see something intriguing every time he passed through the Market ? “I just saw you dancing. I’m willing to trade .” “I don’t dance for bots anymore!” She raised the bar again and took a step forward, ready to swing. “And you have no damned right to spy on me !” Ronin didn’t move. “You left the door open .” “That is not an invitation .” He dipped a hand into his pocket, closing it around the canteen. The woman stiffened, face paling . “What are you doing?” Her voice wavered with uncertainty . Slowly, he withdrew the container and held it up for her to see. Then he leaned across the threshold and placed it on the ground . “What is that ?” “Holds liquid,” he replied. “You know. Water, oil, gasoline. Dust, if you want .” “I don’t trade with bots.” She stared at the canteen . “Just for a dance .” “You already saw one .” Ronin considered her statement. She was right. Nothing was free in this world. And the canteen had some value; it could be traded for a few rounds of ammunition, or leather to repair his boots. There’d be someone in town interested in obtaining it who had something worth Ronin’s while . “Then it is payment for the first. What do you want for another ?” “I told you, I don’t trade with bots. Take it and get out .” Her expression was at odds with her tone, her inner conflict clear on her face . “My name is Ronin. What is yours ?” “Your metal skull that dense that you don’t understand what I said? Fuck off !” He’d done her no wrong — not that he could perceive, anyway — and had never seen her before. He couldn’t guess why she would treat him in such a manner, but it didn’t matter. He was wasting time. If he pushed her to violence, his defensive programming was likely to kick in. It would be too easy to end her, and, despite her hostility, he had no desire to harm this woman . She’d mentioned spying. He reminded himself that humans were particular about having their own, private spaces. Ronin had intruded upon her security . He ran his optics over her a final time. Her shoulders rose and fell with her heavy breaths, the steel bar hovering closer to her waistline now. But there was more to her, something he hadn’t given much attention to before — her imperfections . Those freckles, the light scar on her wrist, the thinness of her frame. Her fingernails were dirty and broken, her hands rough, face smudged with dirt. Synths and humans were virtually identical on the surface. Damage to a synth would alter its appearance slightly, but humans changed so much, so often. They wore their hardships on their bodies, told their stories through their scars. For a bot, damage was often fleeting. Cracked casings could be sealed, burned-out circuits replaced, actuators repaired, synthetic skin refabricated . A synth could be made to look new again. A human, for good or ill, could not . He turned and stepped onto the road . “I said to take your bottle!” she shouted . Ronin didn’t answer. Shrouded in the shadows cast by the Bot District’s electric glow, he headed for the Market without looking back . He replayed the woman’s dance on a loop as he walked, seeking new ways to analyze it, to reduce it to mathematics and discern the obscure, underlying pattern that would unlock true understanding. It was almost as difficult as puzzling out the woman’s mood . Disdain between bots and humans was not uncommon, but she’d displayed something more intense. Despite their physical shortcomings, humans were psychologically complex creatures, and their way of viewing the world was beyond most bots’ ability to comprehend — but just barely . He passed through the open gates and into the Market. It was contained within a large, separate section of the wall, five hundred feet by seven hundred. Eleven pre-Black Out buildings stood within, though it wasn’t likely that their original purposes had survived. Closer to the gate, a patch of cracked asphalt hosted a variety of stalls from which vendors sold their wares . The exposed metal of Ronin’s hands gleamed beneath the white flood lights. He scanned the crowd, easily picking out the humans because of their wavering postures and the sheens of sweat on their imperfect skin . Most of the humans in Cheyenne seemed to return to their homes before the sun set, making the few organics here now the minority. They were largely gathered at the food vendor’s booth, haggling over meals as pots steamed behind the counter. The cook was a bot, a sleek white and red model with basic facial features that only vaguely approximated those of a synth or human. Everyone was made in the image of the Creators, but none had been made equally . Ronin shifted his optics to Kitty’s. The lights on the outside were garish; pink, purple, and blue neon that had somehow weathered the Black Out and now served to lure bots and humans alike into a place where a variety of pleasures could — for a price — be sampled. It made him think of the redhaired human once again . How would she have danced, had she accepted his proposal? He could simulate millions of possibilities, if he chose, but didn’t bother wasting the time or the energy. His processors would never match the reality of her movement . During his time in Cheyenne, he’d never once entered Kitty’s. Hadn’t, in fact, had a woman — metal or meat — in 4,112 days, since long before coming into town . Dancers! Exclaimed one of the signs. Perhaps the redhaired woman was right. He could find what he wanted at Kitty’s, after he saw to his salvage . The scrapper, Zeke, operated out of a large building with few windows. Ronin approached the counter, built immediately outside one of the doors. The scrapper was a tall, thin synth who’d worn the skin off his hands and never deigned to replace it. Considering his profession, it wouldn’t have lasted long, anyway . “Dustwalker. I detected the clang of your pack a mile out.” Zeke’s modulator produced a voice that was deep and rough, somehow perfectly fitted to Cheyenne . Ronin unslung his rifle to swing his pack off his shoulders. Unclasping the flap, he loosened the drawstring and upended the bag. Scrap clattered out onto the scuffed and scratched counter. Zeke watched, expression neutral, as bundles of tangled copper wire bounced over plates of lead and steel, as plastic chips and long-dead power cells clattered over the surface. Plunging a hand into his pocket, Ronin added the ammunition he’d taken from the reavers to the pile . “Never seen one as productive as you,” Zeke said. His hands hovered over the pile, fingers twitching. “You’re a far-rover, to be sure. Creators programmed you special .” “How much?” Ronin asked. His core programming was shrouded within a deep, corrupted memory bank. If the Creators had instilled him with a special purpose, they’d also gone out of their way to hide it from him; he was just another bot with no discernable directive . Zeke sifted through the pile, rubbing and tapping various items and occasionally lifting a piece to test its weight . “Forty units advance. Give me an hour, and I’ll have your full tally .” “ Estimate .” The middle and little fingers on Zeke’s right hand curled for an instant, spasmed, and straightened. “Three fifty. Depends on the damage to the cells, and the precious in the chips .” Nodding, Ronin slung his rucksack over one shoulder and his rifle over the other. Would the woman dance for hard credit ? Zeke counted out the chits — plastic discs with Warlord’s symbol etched at the center, grooves radiating outward from it like spokes on a wheel — and stacked them on the counter. Two yellows and a green made forty units . Ronin slid them off the counter and dropped them into his pocket. Credit units were well enough for now, but they wouldn’t hold any value outside Cheyenne. He’d have to convert them into solid goods before he moved on to another town . “One hour,” he said, walking away from the scrapper’s . He ran his optics over the Market, noting the presence of the merchants he’d need to visit. Forty credits wouldn’t get him much — ammunition was rare, and therefore expensive — and he didn’t care to negotiate prices without the chits in hand . Kitty’s gaudy lights caught his attention again. The recording of the woman dancing rose to the forefront of his attention. An hour spent sating his curiosity couldn’t be considered wasted time. Perhaps it’d be long enough to understand why the redhaired woman had so intrigued him. He walked toward the building . Perhaps it was simply that he’d been in the Dust too long. Diagnostics checks told him his processors were functioning normally, that there was no new corrupted data, but how could he be certain ? It was always his choice to go out into the wasteland and scavenge, at immense risk to his functionality. Often, he was forced to fight. Rarely did those fights end without Ronin having sustained some sort of damage. Yet it was only in those instances, in the chaotic din of combat, that he felt closest to fulfilling his core programming. It had been the same for one hundred and eighty-five years . Why now? Why had the woman’s dance invaded his thoughts so thoroughly ? He stopped at the front door, staring up at the neon sign. He’d not once been tempted to enter before tonight . But change was a natural part of existence for all things. Even mountains changed, infinitesimally, over the eons. Why not bots, as well ? Ronin opened the door and stepped inside. There was a partial wall directly ahead, creating a small foyer and dulling the rhythmic thump of music from beyond. The space was dominated by a broad, blocky bot, at least nine feet tall. Its twin optics audibly shifted to focus on Ronin as the bot raised its thick arms and folded them over its dented metal chest. Warlord’s symbol was clear on its left shoulder, in fresh red paint . Though the source of the knowledge was unclear, Ronin knew this bot had been made for warfare . “Ten units to watch,” it said, voice projecting from somewhere within its suspension-cable neck. It had no moving mouth . One frivolous expenditure couldn’t hurt. It might mean a few less bullets, but he needed to be conservative with ammunition, anyway. Ronin plucked a yellow chit from his pocket and dropped it into the bot’s waiting hand . The bot curled its fingers over the chit. “You the dustwalker the boss mentioned ?” “I’m a dustwalker,” Ronin replied. “Couldn’t tell you which one he meant .” It lifted its head, optic shutters curling small, and released an electronic grunt. “I’m Comp . You — ” “ Comp ?” “Yeah. Short for Compactor. You start trouble, you deal with me. You don’t want that .” “Astute observation .” Comp grunted again and extended a thumb, jabbing it to the side. Ronin walked around the foyer, giving Comp a wide berth, and entered the main room . It took two seconds for his optics to adjust to the confused lighting. More pink and violet bulbs cast conflicting glows upon numerous reflective surfaces, strengthening the contrasting gloom hanging in the air. Mirrors and polished chrome poles and rails gleamed around the room — on the stage, on the bar, around the doors, even on the ceiling . More so than the Bot District, this place was a blatant statement of defiance against the Dust. A callback to an era no one remembered. An era before broken people scurried through a broken world. Before dirt had worked its way into everything, before metal had rusted and corroded and electronic minds deteriorated alongside it . People, metal and organic, were scattered in the chairs. Some sat in small groups at the tables, but most were along the stage. Two synths stood behind the bar, one of them a tall female in a tight dress that accentuated her body. Apart from the pulsing beat of music, the place was quiet. No conversation, no cheering. In one corner, a naked woman writhed sensually on a man’s lap, but the rest of the patrons just stared at the stage with rapt, hungry eyes . Ronin sat at an empty table, turning his optics to the stage . Two women danced at opposite ends, swaying their hips, running their hands over bare skin and across their painted lips, stroking the tips of their nipples . Something stirred within him. He’d gone a long time without sexual stimulation . The woman on the left moved with slow precision. Her skin was flawless, curves generous, breasts perky. And her face was perfectly symmetrical. A synth. She caught his gaze, smiled, and squeezed her ample bosom . Ronin looked to the other woman. In comparison, her movements were erratic, varying in direction and speed. The subtle motion of muscles beneath her skin and the hint of ribs at her sides said she was human. Her breasts were smaller, and pale scars ran up the inside of her right arm. There was a mole on her abdomen, near her navel, and another on her inner thigh . As he watched her, he allocated extra internal resources to mapping her movements. There had to be a pattern, had to be a way to predict them. Minutes passed in the gloom; forgotten, insignificant minutes dominated by droning music and teasing flesh. Units were tossed onto the stage at the dancers’ feet by silent males — and a female, from time to time — disrupting the ambient rhythm . Though there was no predictable pattern, she had several tells. Her movements were simple, sensual, and ultimately repetitive. When she slid her hands down, along her sides, and hesitated for a fraction of a second, it meant they were about to converge over her pubis. If she didn’t hesitate, she would instead run her palms over her thighs . Would the woman in the shack have betrayed such signs, had there been enough time to observe her? He couldn’t know . He analyzed their forms; this woman was slightly taller and seemed better fed, though the redhead in the shack had more muscle tone on her calves. Both had long legs and thin waists . But they danced in drastically different manners . Ronin raised his optics to the dancer’s face. The rare times she opened her eyes, she’d give the crowd only a cursory glance before staring instead at the ceiling or a wall. Her lips were parted slightly, as though in arousal, but it didn’t carry to the rest of her expression. Ronin had seen the way human bodies reacted to sexual stimuli. There was no color on her cheeks, no muscles bunching in response to her pleasure, not so much as a flicker of movement in her passive brow . “I was told you gave my guards trouble when you came into town .” Ronin turned his head. He’d heard no approaching footsteps. Whether it was the music or his focused analysis, his carelessness was a foolish mistake . The bot standing beside Ronin’s table wouldn’t stand out in a crowd. He was a synth of average height and build, hair trimmed to stubble, wearing a faded leather jacket and worn blue jeans. No weapons in sight, no bulked-up frame. His face was neither particularly attractive nor displeasing, save for a single feature — the only feature that set him apart from anyone else. The tear in his synthetic skin, running from his left eye to his jaw, had never been properly repaired. It was held together by thin metal sutures, displaying a glimpse of the faceplates and teeth beneath . “Enough trouble for you to come personally, Warlord?” Ronin returned his attention to the human on stage. “I think your bots have spent too much time with humans. They’ve learned to exaggerate .” At the edge of Ronin’s vision, Warlord pulled out a chair and eased into it, turning his optics to the dancers. “They don’t have many admirable qualities, these sacks of meat…but they can be entertaining .” The human on stage flicked her eyes to Warlord, and her movements faltered . “Yes.” Ronin replayed the redhead’s dance again. If he’d been at a better angle, had a more open view, he could have properly seen the expression on her face . “Good for brief diversions.” Warlord leaned an arm on the table. He tapped his index finger atop it, in time with the music’s beat . Ronin clenched his jaw. Warlord was not to be taken lightly, but the dustwalker rarely sought company. “You find me just to scold me about being late ?” Warlord made a sound almost like a human laugh, but from his mouth, it was hollow and tinny. “Comp said you look like trouble .” “And what do you see ?” “Opportunity. You’ve traveled the Dust. You’ve seen what this world is. And you know the truth of things .” “What truth are you referring to?” Ronin faced his guest , though Warlord didn’t look away from the stage . “The truth of my city. Cheyenne is an oasis in a desert, a sanctuary. I know there are other towns out there, but none are like this place. And that’s because I’ve forged order in a chaotic world. That’s why Cheyenne is still standing. That’s why we have prosperity and comfort .” “Even chaos has an order to it,” Ronin said . Warlord’s finger stilled, and he frowned. “Chaos birthed the world we’re forced to exist within. It’s a world that needs to be tamed. Order brings prosperity, which benefits us all. Even the meatbags .” “What does that have to do with me? Am I an agent of chaos, a thing to be tamed ?” “No. You’re capable. Never had a walker come through who brought in so much scrap so regularly. You come back damaged, sometimes, but you always come back.” Warlord turned his gaze to Ronin; his optics were the gray of old steel. “How many have you ended out there? How many have you left behind to be claimed by the Dust ?” Ronin’s hands twitched on his lap. The faces of every human and bot he’d destroyed were preserved in his memory, frozen eternally in the instant before death or deactivation. He would carry them until his own end, always with perfect clarity. It was the memories he couldn’t place — the faces that drifted up from his damaged core, the ones from before the Black Out — that concerned him most . There were hundreds more of those . “You’re a danger, dustwalker. Not because you’ve killed. Most of us have done that.” Warlord’s finger resumed tapping, slowly but forcefully, no longer with any regard to the rhythm. “You’re a danger because you disregard the rules. The rules are what set this place apart, what raised it above the rest. Otherwise, this placed would be the same as all the other ruins you’ve picked through. Another gravestone in a worldwide cemetery .” “I should have sheltered in the Dust tonight .” “No.” Warlord slammed his fist on the table and leaned forward, scarred face still impassive despite the intensity in his eyes. Several patrons glanced over, looking away quickly. “You should have shown some fucking respect .” “Your guards haven’t earned my respect yet .” “You shelter here in private quarters because I allow it. I offer that to all bots, because we’re the ones who will rebuild this world and make it whole again. All I ask is that my rules are followed. They’re not difficult. All the bots that wear my mark have the authority to enforce those rules. Disrespecting them is disrespecting me .” Ronin forced his jaw closed and temporarily cut power to his vocal modulator to keep any words from escaping. Dozens of bots wore Warlord’s mark. Ronin would never make it out of the Market if Warlord commanded it . “You took some bullets out there,” Warlord said after several seconds, easing back into his chair and swinging his optics back to the dancers. “Behave yourself, so you don’t collect a few more on your way to the Clinic .” Ronin pressed a bare metal hand to the table and pushed himself up. A strange sensation skittered over his palm, and his processors, unbidden, ran a simulation — if he unslung his rifle, he could empty the magazine into Warlord’s chest. Power cell, memory banks, CPU…at this range, it would all be annihilated. The simulation ended with Ronin torn apart by the behemoth at the front door. There weren’t enough bullets in Cheyenne to stop Comp. Still, it was strangely satisfying . He shoved his hands into his coat pockets. Zeke should nearly be done with his assessment, by now. With a last glance at the stage, Ronin left Kitty’s. He couldn’t determine whether the yellow unit for admission had been wasted .
